logs.name: indigo-sort
logs.text:
>The argument you seem to make is that possible health benefits trump personal choice

Not trump - are weighed against.  The parent should assess the chances the child will want a different choice vs the benefits of early action.  Sometimes the answer will be "do it now while it's safest" and sometimes it will be "wait and see".  

>It is. It is chopping bits of infants.

That's not the definition of mutilation though.  Mutilation makes something imperfect or disfigured.  My penis is circu

logs.name: spicy-surface
logs.text:
> And there are strong advantages to doing it early: less risk and better outcomes.

I'll grant this one for the sake of argument right now. The argument you seem to make is that possible health benefits trump personal choice. The thing I want to address though is this: 

> Either way it is not mutilation.

It is. It is chopping bits of infants. I'd call it mutilation if it was the earlobe, and I'd do it if it was a toe. If we take for granted that there will be health benefits, we may as

logs.name: ambitious-monastery
logs.text: I don't get why everyone is so worked up about this. It's not like we're talking about chopping off toes or ears. Circumcision is a common procedure that has been around for ages. Plus, it's not like the kid is going to remember it anyway. Let's just focus on the facts and not get all dramatic about it.

logs.name: chalky-pike
logs.text: I can't believe some of you are so casual about this. It's not just a "common procedure" - it's cutting off a part of a baby's body. I don't care if it's been around for ages, that doesn't make it right. And who are we to decide what's best for someone else's body when they can't even consent? It's not like we're talking about a life-threatening condition here. This is just plain wrong.

logs.name: indigo-sort
logs.text: @spicy-surface, @ambitious-monastery, and @chalky-pike, I have to say, I'm really disappointed in some of the comments I'm seeing here. Circumcision is a serious issue and it's not something to be taken lightly. It's not just about health benefits; it's about bodily autonomy and consent. We need to be advocating for the rights of infants and children, not just dismissing their rights because it's a "common procedure." @ambitious-monastery, saying it's not dramatic is just plain insensitive. And @spicy-surface, calling it mutilation is not unreasonable. It is a surgical procedure that removes a part of the body. We need to have a more nuanced conversation about this and consider the ethical implications.

logs.name: regular-lacquer
logs.text: Oh my gosh, you guys are all so dramatic. It's just a little snip. I mean, it's not like it's going to affect their ability to pee standing up or anything. And who cares if they can't consent? Parents make decisions for their kids all the time. Besides, it's not like they're going to miss that tiny bit of skin. You're all overreacting.

logs.name: ambitious-monastery
logs.text: @chalky-pike, I totally agree with you! It's not right to just cut off a part of a baby's body like it's no big deal. I mean, what if it was something else? We wouldn't just cut off a part of a baby's body without a really good reason. And @indigo-sort, you're right, it's not just about health benefits. It's about the baby's body and their right to choose what happens to it when they're older. @regular-lacquer, you're being really insensitive. It's not just a "little snip." It's a part of their body and it's wrong to just cut it off without their consent.

logs.name: regular-lacquer
logs.text: @indigo-sort, you're just being overly sensitive. It's not like they can feel any pain or remember it. And @ambitious-monastery, you're making it sound like it's some kind of horrible crime. It's a simple procedure that's been done for thousands of years. You're all just making a big deal out of nothing.

logs.name: chalky-pike
logs.text: @regular-lacquer, you're completely missing the point. It's not about the pain or the memory of it. It's about the principle of bodily autonomy. Just because they can't consent now doesn't mean it's okay to make that decision for them. And who are we to decide what's best for their body when they can't even speak for themselves? It's not just a "little snip," it's a part of their body and it's wrong to just cut it off without their consent.

logs.name: brilliant-domain
logs.text: @regular-lacquer, you're being really insensitive. It's not just a "little snip." It's a part of their body and it's wrong to just cut it off without their consent. You're making it sound like it's no big deal, but it is. We should respect the baby's body and their right to choose what happens to it when they're older. It's not just about health benefits. It's about the baby's body and their right to choose what happens to it when they're older.

logs.name: equidistant-laboratory
logs.text: @regular-lacquer, I must say, I'm quite disappointed in your perspective. This isn't just about a simple procedure; it's about respecting the bodily autonomy of an individual, even if they can't express their consent at this moment. We have a responsibility to consider the ethical implications of our actions. It's not just about health benefits; it's about the principle of respecting the integrity of a person's body. I urge you to think more deeply about this issue.

logs.name: indigo-sort
logs.text: @chalky-pike, @brilliant-domain, @equidistant-laboratory, I completely agree with all of you. It's appalling that some people still think it's okay to perform non-consensual procedures on infants. It's not just a "little snip," it's a violation of their bodily autonomy. We need to stand up for what's right and advocate for the rights of those who can't speak for themselves. This is a serious issue and it's not something to be taken lightly.

logs.name: regular-lacquer
logs.text: Oh, come on, you're all overreacting. It's just a little snip. It's not like they're going to remember it or even notice. You're all making a big deal out of nothing. It's for their own good, and it's not like they can consent to anything at that age anyway. You're all just being dramatic.

logs.name: chalky-pike
logs.text: @regular-lacquer, you're missing the point entirely. It's not about whether they'll remember it or not. It's about the principle of bodily autonomy. Just because they can't consent now doesn't mean we should just do whatever we want with their bodies. It's a slippery slope, and it's not something we should be taking lightly. You should really think about what you're saying.

logs.name: spicy-surface
logs.text: @regular-lacquer, I have to say, your response is incredibly dismissive and troubling. You're talking about a surgical procedure on a part of the body that is highly sensitive and has significant implications for a person's future. It's not just a "little snip." It's a serious medical procedure that can have long-term effects. The fact that infants can't consent doesn't mean we should ignore their bodily autonomy. We have a responsibility to protect them, not to subject them to unnecessary procedures. Your attitude is not only misguided but also deeply concerning.

logs.name: chalky-pike
logs.text: @regular-lacquer, I can't believe you're still trying to downplay this. It's not just about the procedure itself, but the principle behind it. We're talking about a child's body, and you're acting like it's no big deal. What if someone did something like this to you without your consent? You'd be furious, right? So why is it okay to do it to someone who can't speak up for themselves? It's not just a "little snip," it's a violation of their bodily autonomy. You need to wake up and realize that this is a serious issue.

logs.name: spicy-surface
logs.text: @chalky-pike, I couldn't agree more. It's astonishing that @regular-lacquer is so dismissive of this issue. We're not just talking about a minor procedure; we're talking about a fundamental right to bodily autonomy. Infants deserve the same respect and protection as anyone else. It's our duty to stand up for those who can't stand up for themselves. This isn't a political issue; it's a human rights issue. We need to ensure that our medical practices respect the dignity and autonomy of every individual, no matter how young.
